elements.sampleAnswer1: Manyan abubuwa shida da Allah ya halitta: (1) haske da rabuwar rana da dare; (2) sama da rabuwar ruwa; (3) ∆ôasa mai bushe, teku, da tsirrai; (4) rana, wata, da taurari; (5) halittu na teku da tsuntsaye; (6) dabbobin ∆ôasa da ‚Äôyan adam. Tsari mai ∆ôarfi ya fito a cikin kwanakin shida: kwanaki uku na farko sun tsara muhallin ko ‚Äôsarari‚Äô (haske, sama, ∆ôasa/teku), kuma kwanakin uku masu zuwa sun cika wa…óannan sarari da mazaunansu masu dacewa (fitilu sun cika sama, kifi da tsuntsaye sun cika teku da sama, dabbobi da ‚Äôyan adam sun cika ∆ôasa). Kowace rana tana ∆ôarewa da ‚ÄôAllah ya ga cewa yana da kyau‚Äô, kuma jerin duka sun ∆ôare da ‚Äômai kyau ∆ôwarai‚Äô. Tsarin yana tafiya daga rashin siffa da babu komai zuwa da tsari da cike ‚Äî yana nuna halitta a matsayin da niyya, da tsari, da manufa.
elements.questionHint1: Ka duba kwanaki 1-3 sannan kwanaki 4-6 gefe da gefe ‚Äî shin kuna lura da alaka tsakanin abin da aka halicci a rana ta 1 da rana ta 4, rana ta 2 da rana ta 5, rana ta 3 da rana ta 6? Wannan yin biyu yana nuna menene game da tsarin labarun?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Kuuje joweego maw…óe …óe Allah tagi: (1) ndaygu e seebaare hannde e kiikiide; (2) dow e seebaare ndiyam; (3) leydi toow…ói, maayo e riiweendi; (4) naange, lewru e taaraabiiji; (5) taguuji ndiyam e tii…óo; (6) taguuji leydi e yim…ìe. Mbadi haarii…ói …óon yalta nder bal…óe joweego …óen: bal…óe tati fu…ó…óoode …óen …óon sifaa yoorno‚Äôen koo ‚Äô…óoyngal‚Äô (ndaygu, dow, leydi/maayo), nden bal…óe tati go…ó…óe …óen …óon hisana yoorno‚Äôen …óen e keerol ma…ì…ìe …óen (fitiilaaji …óon hiisana dow, li…ó…ói e tii…óo …óon hiisana maayo e dow, taguuji e yim…ìe …óon hiisana leydi). Kala …óoon nyalaande …óon ∆¥etoo e ‚ÄôAllah yi‚Äôi …óum woni bo…ó…óum‚Äô, nden kala nganaaka …óon wi…ó…óitoo e ‚Äôbo…ó…óum sanne‚Äô. Mbadi …óon ya≈ãa daga walaa sifaa e baaw…óe haa e diidol e heblii…óol ‚Äî hollitde tagu gaa o wa…ói bee nuun…óal, diidol, e yi…óo.
elements.questionHint2: Yiy bal…óe 1-3 nden bal…óe 4-6 taa…ói ‚Äî a…óa yii ko woodi jokki tawi ko tagaa nyalaande 1 e nyalaande 4, nyalaande 2 e nyalaande 5, nyalaande 3 e nyalaande 6? Ko woni ko …óim…ìinde …ói…ói …óuye hollata dow sifaa labaari ngari ngun?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Six main things God created: (1) light and the separation of day and night; (2) the sky and the separation of waters; (3) dry land, seas, and vegetation; (4) the sun, moon, and stars; (5) sea creatures and birds; (6) land animals and human beings. A clear pattern emerges across the six days: the first three days form the environments or ‚Äôspaces‚Äô (light, sky, land/sea), and the next three days fill those spaces with their corresponding inhabitants (luminaries fill the sky, fish and birds fill sea and sky, animals and humans fill the land). Each day ends with ‚ÄôGod saw that it was good‚Äô, and the whole sequence concludes with ‚Äôvery good‚Äô. The pattern moves from formless and empty to ordered and filled ‚Äî showing creation as deliberate, structured, and purposeful.
elements.questionHint3: Look at days 1-3 and then days 4-6 side by side ‚Äî do you notice a relationship between what is created on day 1 and day 4, day 2 and day 5, day 3 and day 6? What does that pairing suggest about the structure of the account?

elements.sampleAnswer3: The repeated pattern of ‚ÄôGod said... and it was so‚Äô throughout Genesis 1 shows that God‚Äôs word alone is sufficient to bring anything into existence. He does not need materials, effort, tools, or assistance ‚Äî He simply speaks and reality responds instantly and completely. This tells us that God‚Äôs authority over creation is absolute: the created world does not exist independently of Him but is entirely subject to His command. No human being, no force of nature, and nothing in the universe has power comparable to God‚Äôs ‚Äî creation came into being by His word, continues to exist by His sustaining power, and answers to Him entirely.
elements.questionHint3: When a human being gives an order, it might or might not be obeyed ‚Äî but when God speaks in Genesis 1, what always happens immediately, and what does that instant, total response tell you about the kind of authority He has?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Bayyanar ‚Äômai kyau ∆ôwarai‚Äô yana gaya mana cewa duniya kamar yadda Allah Ya ∆ôir∆ôira ta ba ta da lahani, tana karye, ko tana da tsaka tsaki ta …óabi‚Äôa ‚Äî ta yi daidai da niyya ta Allah kuma ta nuna halinsA. elements.sampleAnswer2: Wolwude ‚Äôbo…ó…óum sanne‚Äô …óon hollita en ko duuniyaaru hono Allah tagiri nde fu…ó…óoode waayi duuniyaaru ko walaa aibu, ko kar…óaaki, koo naa ko walaa nuun…óal ‚Äî nde wa…óii daidaari e nuun…óal Allah nden hollitii sifa mum. Ngam majjum …óon woodiri heewi ngam no min miijorto dow duuniyaaru jonta, ngam …óum …óon wa…óa en anndan ko cew…ói, bonii…ói e ko boni min yi…ói yi‚Äôde waawaa gaa tagaama …óum fuu ‚Äî …ìe wa…ói taa…ìal goppu…óo, walaa …óum tagiraayi hono ndeen. Ngam majjum …óon hokka en dalil ngam gullande ko bonii nden miilinde ko Allah seli e su…ìtinaade welto ‚Äî …óum kadi hollita min mbaawa zallude Allah dow ko duuniyaaru jonta woni.
elements.questionHint2: Si Allah wi‚Äôi tagu fu…ó…óoode ‚Äôbo…ó…óum sanne‚Äô ammaa duuniyaaru min yi…ói yi‚Äôde jonta …óon wada weloore e bonande, ko woni ko ndiyam …ói…óam …óuye hollata dow ko Allah tagiri …óum hono ndeen, koo si ko …óume bonii caggal?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The declaration ‚Äôvery good‚Äô tells us that the world as God originally made it was not flawed, broken, or morally neutral ‚Äî it was perfectly aligned with God‚Äôs intention and reflected His character. There was no suffering, no death, no corruption. This matters enormously for how we think about the world now, because it means the pain, brokenness, and evil we see are not original features of creation ‚Äî they are intrusions. The world as it now is has been damaged, not designed that way. This gives us reason to grieve what has been lost and hope for what God has promised to restore ‚Äî it also means we cannot blame God for the state of the world as it currently exists.
elements.questionHint3: If God called the original creation ‚Äôvery good‚Äô but the world we see today contains suffering and brokenness, what does that difference suggest about whether God designed things this way, or whether something has gone wrong since?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Bisa ga wannan aya, halitta tana yin manufar bayyana …óaukakar Allah da nuna ayyukansa. Sammai ba shaidun shiru ba ne ‚Äî suna shelar wani abu game da Mahaliccinsu. Halitta, a wani ma‚Äôana, tana ba da shaida ta dindindin, ta duniya, ga wanzuwar Allah, ∆ôarfinSa, da kyaunsA, wadda kowane mutum a kowace zamanin da al‚Äôada zai iya samu. Mutane da yawa suna gano cewa lokuttan da ke cikin duniyar halitta ‚Äî sararin sama mai yawa da daren, tsaunin tudun, bakin teku, ban mamaki na wata halittar mai rai ‚Äî suna haifar da jin tsoron Allah wanda a hankali yana nuna wuce abin kansa zuwa ga wanda Ya ∆ôir∆ôira shi. elements.questionHint1: Zabura yana cewa sammai ‚Äôsuna shelar‚Äô wani abu ‚Äî kamar dai halitta tana sadar da sa∆ôo mai aiki. Menene sa∆ôon da take shelarsa, kuma shin kun taba ganin kanku kuna jin wannan sa∆ôon lokacin da kuke kalloni wani abu a duniyar halitta?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Bana no ayaare nde, tagu …óon wa…óa kuugal ngal hollitde teddungal Allah e hollitde taa…ìal juu…óe mum. Dow …óon walaa shayiduuji muumii…ìe ‚Äî …ìe noldata ko …óume e Tagotoowo ma…ì…ìe. Tagu, e laawol go…ó…óo, woni seedtere nde …óon e keewal, nde jaajde, ngam woodugol Allah, semmbe mum e mbele mum, nde kala ne…ó…óo e kala sahaa e kala senngo mbaawa yiyde. Yim…ìe heew…ìe …óon yiytoo ko nder dakiika …óe nder duuniyaaru tag…óo ‚Äî dow faatiraa…óo e kiikiide, duwanyi maw…ói, kenngol maayo, yi…ótaari tagu…óo wuuru…óo ‚Äî …óon wa…óa en miilanaago majjum ko yawtii …óum haa Mo tagiri …óum. Ko nden nden nden nden Jabuuraajo …óon wi‚Äôi tagu wa…óaama ngam wa…óde.
elements.questionHint2: Jabuura …óon wi‚Äôa dow …óon ‚Äônoldata‚Äô ko …óume ‚Äî hono nde nde tagu …óon wolwi fow habaaru. Ko woni habaaru ngo …óon nolditee, nden a…óa waawi siftorde wakkati mo a yi‚Äôi …óum a miilanaago …óum nde a yi‚Äôi ko …óume nder duuniyaaru tag…óo?
elements.sampleAnswer3: According to this verse, creation serves the purpose of declaring God‚Äôs glory and displaying His handiwork. The heavens are not silent witnesses ‚Äî they actively proclaim something about their Maker. Creation is, in a sense, a constant, universal testimony to God‚Äôs existence, power, and beauty, available to every person in every age and culture. Many people find that moments in the natural world ‚Äî a vast night sky, a mountain range, a coastline, the intricacy of a living creature ‚Äî produce a sense of awe that points instinctively beyond the thing itself to the one who made it. This is precisely what the psalmist says creation is designed to do.
elements.questionHint3: The psalm says the heavens ‚Äôdeclare‚Äô something ‚Äî as if creation is actively communicating a message. What is the message it is declaring, and have you ever found yourself sensing that message when looking at something in the natural world?

A aikace, ba game da wani aiki mai ban mamaki guda …óaya ba amma game da alkiblar rayuwa gaba …óaya: za…ìu…ì…ìukan da muke yi, yadda muke bi da mutane, abubuwan da muke nema da ba da fifiko, da kuma ko rayuwarmu tana nuna wa sauran hanyar zuwa ga Allah ko ta nisantar da su. Nder taa…ìal, …óum walaa e taa…ìal juumtaa…óo go‚Äôo tan ammaa e √±ii…ìdoore wuurnam gaa fuu: su…ìe min njokkata, no min mbaawa yim…ìe, …óume min njokkata e min njokkita e …óume min ndokkata yeeso, nden kadi ko wuurnam mee…óen kollita yim…ìe laawol haa Allah koo yaltinaata …ìe. Tawa en tagaama e sifa Allah (Fu…ó…óoode 1:27), wuurde ngam teddungal mum …óon kadi wa…óa hollitde sifa mum ‚Äî yi…ói mum, tii…ói mum, innitare mum e suu…óo mum ‚Äî nder kuu…óe mee…óen nde kowa nde. Sabu wuurnam mee…óen, bana Ishaya wolwi, walaa e he…ìde he…ìtinde hoore mee…óen ammaa e wuurde e maayanaago Allah.
elements.questionHint2: Si tagaama ‚Äôngam teddungal Allah‚Äô …óon wa…óa wuurnam mee…óen taraani kollinaade Mo, ko woni ko woni …óe ne…ó…óo mbaawi wa…óde ‚Äî koo naa wa…óanaa wa…óde ‚Äî nder kuu…óe nde kowa nde …óe nde mbaawi hollinaade koo waylitaade sabu ngam majjum?
elements.sampleAnswer3: To live for God‚Äôs glory means to live in a way that makes God look great ‚Äî that draws attention to His character, His goodness, and His worth rather than to ourselves. In practice, this is not about a single dramatic act but about the orientation of an entire life: the choices we make, the way we treat people, the things we pursue and prioritise, and whether our lives point others towards God or away from Him. Since we are made in God‚Äôs image (Genesis 1:27), living for His glory also means reflecting His character ‚Äî His love, justice, faithfulness, and compassion ‚Äî in our everyday lives. The purpose of our existence, according to Isaiah, is not self-fulfilment but God-centred living.
elements.questionHint3: If being created ‚Äôfor God‚Äôs glory‚Äô means our lives are meant to point to Him, what kinds of things might a person do ‚Äî or avoid doing ‚Äî in ordinary daily life that would either reflect or contradict that purpose?

elements.sampleAnswer1: An halicci mutum cikin surar Allah yana nufin cewa ‚Äôyan adam sun nuna wani abu na halin Allah na gaskiya ‚Äî tunaninsA, ma‚Äôanar …óabi‚ÄôarSa, ∆ôarfinSa don dangantaka, ∆ôir∆ôira, da ∆ôauna. Babu wata halittar da aka bayyana haka a Farawa. Ana halicci dabbobi ‚Äôbisa ga irin su‚Äô, amma ana halicci ‚Äôyan adam bisa ga surar da kamannin Allah Himself, wanda ke sa mu cikin wata rukunin gaba …óaya. Yana nufin ba kawai mu halittu ne na halitta ba amma mutane na mutumci da an tsara su don dangantaka da Allah, masu iyawar saninsA, wakilcinsA a cikin duniya, da kuma a ri∆ôe da su da lissafin …óabi‚Äôa a gare Shi. elements.sampleAnswer2: Tagaama bee nanndola Allah …óon wa…óa yim…ìe …óon hollitata ko …óume nder sifa Allah e hoore mum ‚Äî annditaare mum, nuun…óal …óabi‚Äôa mum, semmbe mum ngam jokkirde, ∆¥eewre e yi…ói. Walaa tagu…óo go…ó…óo nder Fu…ó…óoode hollirtee hono …óum. Taguuji …óon tagaa ‚Äôbana kaaje mum‚Äô, ammaa yim…ìe tagaa bana sifa e nanndola Allah Hoore mum, nde wa…ói en nder ngenaaji go…ó…ói he…ìi-he…ìi. Ngam majjum …óon wa…óa en walaa e jokooji hoodere tan ammaa en yim…ìe jolnitoo…ìe tagaama ngam jokkirde e Allah, …ói mbaawi annditaade Mo, hollinaade Mo nder duuniyaaru, nden kadi en mbaawi dogirde biro mum. ∆äum kadi …óon wa…óa en anndan wuurnam kala yim…ìe woodi tiindi ma…ì…ìe nde yaltaani, ngam sifa Allah woni nde …óon …óa…ì…ìirte e ne…ó…óo fuu.
elements.questionHint2: Fu…ó…óoode …óon hollita taguuji tagaa ‚Äôbana kaaje mum‚Äô ammaa …óon hollita yim…ìe hano go…ó…óo ‚Äî tagaa bee sifa e nanndola Allah Hoore mum. Ko woni ko bayyan go…ó…óo …óo hollita dow hono jokkirde nde Allah yi…ói nde jokkirde nde o ya…ói yim…ìe bee taguuji luttu…ói fuu?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Being made in the image of God means that human beings uniquely reflect something of God‚Äôs own nature ‚Äî His rationality, His moral sense, His capacity for relationship, creativity, and love. No other creature in Genesis is described this way. The animals are made ‚Äôaccording to their kinds‚Äô, but human beings are made according to God‚Äôs own image and likeness, which places us in an entirely different category. It means we are not merely biological organisms but personal beings designed for relationship with God, capable of knowing Him, representing Him in the world, and being held morally accountable to Him. It also means that every human life has an inherent dignity that cannot be stripped away, because it is God‚Äôs image that is borne in each person.
elements.questionHint3: Genesis describes animals being made ‚Äôaccording to their kinds‚Äô but describes humans differently ‚Äî made in the image and likeness of God Himself. What does that different description suggest about the kind of relationship God intends to have with human beings compared to the rest of creation?

elements.text1: An halicce mu acikin kamannin Allah. Haka nan fa, Allah ya halicci mutum cikin siffarsa, cikin siffar Allah, Allah ya halicci mutum, namiji da ta mace ya halicce su. Farawa 1:27
elements.text2: Goonga woni tagu mee…óen bee nanndola Allah …óon seedintina yim…ìe bee taguuji duuniyaaru luttu…ói. ∆äum …óon holla min wonaa min njalti tan bee masiibe koo sifaaji …ìanduum ‚Äî min taguuji wa…óaa…ói ngam jamanu bee Allah, mo waawi hollaade sifaaji maako nder mbuurnam mee…óen.
elements.text3: The fact that we are made in God‚Äôs image sets human beings apart from the rest of creation. It means we are not simply the product of chance or biology ‚Äî we are creatures made for relationship with God, capable of reflecting His character in the way we live.

elements.sampleAnswer1: Ayyukan biyu sune: (1) mulkin halitta ‚Äî yin mulki da kula a madadin Allah a matsayin wakilansa a duniya; da kuma (2) kula da kulawa da kiyayewa ‚Äî yaren Farawa 2:15 na mai gonar ko mai kula, yana noma da kiyaye abin da aka ba shi amana. Wa…óannan sun ha…óu, ba sun sabawa juna ba: an ba ikon mulkin a cikin mahallin kula mai alhakin, ba cin zarafi ba. Game da yadda muke cika wa…óannan ayyuka a yau ‚Äî mutane da yawa za su fa…ói a gaskiya cewa lalatar muhalli mai ya…óuwa, asarar nau‚Äôin halittu, da cin zarafi na albarkatun halitta yana nuna gazawar sarrafa da Allah Ya nufa. Umarnin mulki ba taba kasancewa izni ba ne na lalata; shi ne kira na hidima ga halitta a madadin Allah.
elements.questionHint1: Farawa 1:26 yana ba ‚Äôyan adam ikon ‚Äômulki a kan‚Äô halitta, yayin da Farawa 2:15 ke cewa an sanya mutumin a cikin lambun don ‚Äôya nomata, ya kuma lura da ita‚Äô ‚Äî shin wa…óannan ra‚Äôayoyin guda biyu suna gane muku kamar abu guda …óaya, ko suna ∆ôara girma daban daban ga yadda sarrafa mai alhakin yake kama da shi?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Kuuje …ói…ói …óuye …óen ngoni: (1) laamude dow tagu ‚Äî wa…óde laamu e rewirde e nder tagu ngam Allah hono wakiiluu…ìe mum nder duuniyaaru; nden (2) rokkirde e ka…ó…óude ‚Äî wolde Fu…ó…óoode 2:15 woni nde nde gardotoowo koo naa ndema-ndemii…óo, nde nelata e ka…ó…óudande ko njokkaa maako bee amana. Kuuje …ói…ói …óuye …óen …óon ha…óa, walaa …óen …óon sabu e yeeso: laamu …óon hokkaama nder catal rokkirde e …óa…ì…ìirgal, walaa nder cinngal. Dow no min mbaawa mballitaade kuuje …óuye jonta ‚Äî yim…ìe heew…ìe mbaawi wi‚Äôde e goonga ko …ìonn‚Äôude muu…óol ngol ya…óetee, bonde kaaje e cinngal alaajiiji leydi …óon hollita ko ngaaliidam garo…óam e gardagol ngal Allah yi…ói. Umarni ngal laamude waayi tabitirde walaa yaltinataake; …óum woni we∆¥e ngel rokkirde tagu ngam Allah.
elements.questionHint2: Fu…ó…óoode 1:26 …óon hokka yim…ìe baaw…óe ngam ‚Äôlaamude dow‚Äô tagu, nde Fu…ó…óoode 2:15 …óon wi‚Äôa ne…ó…óo …óon neli nder lammburu ngam ‚Äôrokkirde nde nden ka…ó…óude‚Äô ‚Äî a…óa miijorto ko …ói…ói …óuye …ói √±aawantaa ka hono fuu …óum koo naa …ìe …ìura hollitde …óim…ìinde ngata nde no gardagol jolnitaa…óo wa‚Äôi?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The two responsibilities are: (1) to rule over creation ‚Äî exercising authority and oversight on God‚Äôs behalf as His representatives in the world; and (2) to care for and maintain it ‚Äî the language of Genesis 2:15 is that of a gardener or steward, tending and preserving what has been entrusted. These are complementary, not contradictory: the authority to rule is given in the context of responsible care, not exploitation. As for how well we are fulfilling these responsibilities today ‚Äî many would argue honestly that the widespread environmental damage, species loss, and exploitation of natural resources reflects a failure of the stewardship God intended. The mandate to rule was never a licence to destroy; it was a call to serve creation on God‚Äôs behalf.
elements.questionHint3: Genesis 1:26 gives humans authority to ‚Äôrule over‚Äô creation, while Genesis 2:15 says the man was placed in the garden to ‚Äôcare for it and maintain it‚Äô ‚Äî do those two ideas feel like the same thing to you, or do they add different dimensions to what responsible stewardship looks like?

elements.sampleAnswer3: The writer paints a picture of life as fundamentally empty and futile ‚Äî all human activity and striving amounts to ‚Äôchasing after wind‚Äô, which is a vivid image for pursuing something that can never be caught or held. Verse 15 deepens the bleakness: what is bent cannot be straightened, and what is missing cannot even be fully counted. In other words, the world is broken in ways that human effort cannot fix and human wisdom cannot fully diagnose. Many people find this description uncomfortably honest ‚Äî it matches the nagging sense that however much we achieve, something remains deeply unsatisfying about life lived without reference to God.
elements.questionHint3: The phrase ‚Äôchasing after wind‚Äô describes trying to catch something ‚Äî what is the point the writer is making about whether that chase can ever succeed, and do you think that rings true of how people experience life?

elements.questionHint2: Yeesu wi‚Äôi duuniyaaru wanyiiyam e hoore maako ngam o seedatii dow kuu…óe mayru ‚Äî ngam …óume wi‚Äôugo goonga dow nguurndam ne…ó…óo wa…óata mo yi…óugo wanyude ko …ìuri yettugo?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says the world‚Äôs deeds are evil, and that this honest testimony is precisely the reason the world hates Him. He is not speaking as someone who hates the world, but as one who tells the truth about it ‚Äî and the truth is unwelcome. The hatred is a reaction to exposure: people who are comfortable with their way of living do not want someone to name it as evil. This is a pattern we recognise: we tend to dislike people who hold a mirror up to behaviour we would rather not examine. The world‚Äôs hostility to Jesus is therefore itself a kind of evidence that His assessment is accurate.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says the world hates Him specifically because He testifies about its deeds ‚Äî why might telling the truth about someone‚Äôs behaviour cause them to react with hostility rather than gratitude?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that the whole of creation has been subjected to futility and is in bondage to decay ‚Äî it is not functioning as it was originally intended to. The language of groaning and suffering captures the brokenness we see in the natural world: disease, death, disaster, and deterioration. Crucially, Paul says this happened not willingly but as a consequence of human sin ‚Äî creation was dragged into the Fall. However, the passage is not without hope: creation is not just suffering, it is waiting and longing. It eagerly anticipates the day when God‚Äôs children are fully revealed and creation itself is set free from decay into a new kind of glorious freedom.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says creation was subjected to futility ‚Äônot willingly‚Äô ‚Äî what does that tell you about whether the brokenness in the natural world is creation‚Äôs own fault or a consequence of something human beings did?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Ba a san tumaki da fitinar da suke yi ba ‚Äî suna …ìata. Ba sa tafiya da husuma; kawai suna yawo, suna bin duk abin da ya jawo hankalinsu a lokacin, ba su san yadda suka nange daga makiyayi ba kuma ba su sani irin ha…óarin da suke ciki ba. Wannan kwatanci yana da ma‚Äôana game da zunubi: mafi yawansa ba taurin kai ne ba sai dai a hankali ba tare da hankali ba ana tafiya daga Allah. ‚ÄôYa juya hanyarsa‚Äô na nufin kowane mutum ya tsayar da hanyarsa ba ya bin Allah ‚Äî ya mai da kansa iko akan yadda za a rayu. Kai tsaye maimakon Allah tsaye, wanda Littafi Mai Tsarki ke ganin shi ne kashin zunubi.
elements.questionHint1: Tumaki ba sa tawayen da aka sa wa ido a hankali ‚Äî kawai suna yawo a hankali ba su fahimci yadda suke …ìacewa. Ta yaya irin wannan tafiyar hankali take kwatanta da yadda kake/kike tunanin zunubi yawanci, kuma shin yana canza fahimtar ka/ki game da shi?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Baali naa‚Äô…ìe nganndinaa bee halkiinde sikkunde ‚Äî …óe …óon njaha. ∆äe ngalaa ko …óe nga…óa bee yiite; …óe …óon njaha tan, …óe ngo…ó…óoo ko yi…óiri …óe nder sahaa nde, …óe annduaa no …óe njaatinike daga gaardii…óo e no su…óo …óe nder ngol. Ko misal goongol dow junuuba: heewol junuuba naa‚Äô ko halkiinde toownde tan, ammaa ko yaadu ha…ì…ìundaa…óo haa…ìundaaki daga nder Allah. ‚ÄôHuccugo laawol mum‚Äô ma‚Äôanti ko ne…ó…óo fuu yewtiti laawol muu…óum e wiide Allah ‚Äî wa…óii hoore maako …ìurnoowo ha…ì…ìingol dow ko wa…óiraa. Ko hoore mum faalitata e ko Allah faalitata, nde Binndol Cenii…óol naata ko nguurngu junuuba.
elements.questionHint2: Baali naa‚Äô…ìe ngalaa ko …óe nga…óa bee halkiinde toownde ‚Äî …óe …óon njaha ha…ì…ìundaaki boo ngannduaa no …óe ngoo…ói ndi …óon yalta. No yaadu ngolu e nannduri bee no a miijataa junuuba tigi, e …óum accita faamde maa dow …óum?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sheep are not known for dramatic rebellion ‚Äî they wander. They do not storm off defiantly; they simply drift, following whatever attracts them in the moment, unaware of how far they have strayed from the shepherd and how much danger they are in. This is a telling image for sin: much of it is not dramatic wickedness but a gradual, unremarkable drifting away from God. To have ‚Äôturned his own way‚Äô means each person has set their own course rather than following God‚Äôs ‚Äî making themselves the authority on how to live. It is self-direction rather than God-direction, which the Bible understands as the essence of sin.
elements.questionHint3: Sheep don‚Äôt usually rebel dramatically ‚Äî they just wander gradually without realising how lost they are becoming. How does that kind of slow drift compare to the way you might normally picture sin, and does it change your understanding of it?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Wannan aya tana bayyana cewa zunubin …óan adam yana da dagewa mai ∆ôarfi: duk da an cece su ‚Äôsau da yawa,‚Äò mutanen sun koma kuma kuma da yawa ga halin taurin kai. Zunubi ba kawai jerin mawuyacin za…ìuka ne ba ‚Äî aya tana bayyana yanayi na asali na taurin kai wanda yakan sake fitowa. Amma aya ta bayyana cewa martanin Allah yana da dagewa kamar haka: ya cece su ‚Äôsau da yawa,‚Äò ba sau …óaya ba. Bai yar da su bayan karo na farko, ko na biyu, ko na goma ba. Tsari a gefen …óan adam ∆ôasawa ce ta maimaita; tsarin a gefen Allah ceto ne na maimaita. Aya ta kuma lura da raunin da zunubi ke yi ‚Äî yana rage mutane, ba kawai ta fuskar …óabi‚Äôa ba amma gaba …óayansu.
elements.questionHint1: Aya tana cewa Allah ya cece su ‚Äôsau da yawa‚Äô ‚Äî me kalmar ‚Äôyawa‚Äô ke nuna game da yadda Allah ya yi ha∆ôuri da gazawarsu ta maimaita, kuma tsarin ceto na maimaita yana gaya maka/ki menene game da halinsa?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Ayaare nde hollata junuuba ne…ó…óo …ìuuri ha…ì…ìude: kono …ìe hisnaa…ìe ‚Äôheewi laabi,‚Äò yim…ìe …ìe …ìeydinii e …ìeydinii yaltude e ngorsi halkiinde. Junuuba naa‚Äô ko …ìooyum laabi su…ìa…ìe kallu…óe tan ‚Äî ayaare nde hollata yonki halkiinde gon…óo nder yaadu woni …óon yaltirta hoore. Kono ayaare boo hollata jaabori Allah woni e ha…ì…ìude hay ko nandori: o hisnii …ìe ‚Äôheewi laabi‚Äô, naa‚Äô laabi gooto tan. O accaaki …ìe caggal faandere aranere, naa‚Äô e …ìolaa…óe, naa‚Äô e sappo. Nguurngu nder ba…óal ne…ó…óo woni faandude caggal caggal; nguurngu nder ba…óal Allah woni hisnaade laabi e laabi. Ayaare boo mii…óii teddingol junuuba wa…óata ‚Äî …óum to…ó…óinirta yim…ìe on, naa‚Äô ha…ì…ìo tan, ammaa ko fuu …ìe ngoni.
elements.questionHint2: Ayaare nde wi‚Äôi Allah hisnii …ìe ‚Äôheewi laabi‚Äô ‚Äî …óume mbele ‚Äôheewi‚Äô hollata dow no Allah sa…ì…ìori faandere ma…ì…ìe caggal caggal, e no hisnaa…óo laabi e laabi hollata no o woni?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The verse reveals that human sin is stubbornly persistent: despite being delivered ‚Äômany times‚Äô, the people returned again and again to a rebellious attitude. Sin is not merely a series of isolated bad choices ‚Äî the verse describes an underlying disposition of rebellion that keeps reasserting itself. But the verse equally reveals that God‚Äôs response is just as persistent: He delivered them ‚Äômany times‚Äô, not once. He did not give up after the first failure, or the second, or the tenth. The pattern on the human side is repeated failure; the pattern on God‚Äôs side is repeated rescue. The verse also notes the degrading effect of sin ‚Äî it diminishes the people, not just morally but in their whole condition.
elements.questionHint3: The verse says God delivered them ‚Äômany times‚Äô ‚Äî what does the word ‚Äômany‚Äô suggest about how patient God was with their repeated failures, and what does the pattern of repeated rescue tell you about His character?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Allah ya ba da izini mai karamci ‚Äî mutumin zai iya cin ‚Äôya‚Äôyan kowane itacen gonar da yardar rai ‚Äî tare da haramci …óaya a sarari: ya kamata kada ya ci daga itacen sanin nagarta da mugunta. elements.sampleAnswer2: Allah hokki yamiroore yaajunde ‚Äî gorko on waawi nyaami …ìi…ì…ìe le…ó…óe fuu nder laabu nguu bee farta≈ã≈ãe ‚Äî fawaade e haramtaare gooto sanne: o foti nyaama daga le…ó…óe gande ko woo…ói e ko kallu…ói. Yamiroore nde naa‚Äô …ìurtii go…ó…óum e …ìurgol; leetel haramtee…óel boo gonngo nder teddungol tan…óingol heewngol. Sa…óaari nde nattiraama e to…ó…óondirugol yamiroore nde woni mayde: ‚Äòlaa…ìi-laa…ìi a mayraa.‚Äô Naa‚Äô cu…óal tan kono haala sa…óaari ‚Äî yartugo iyol ngol na‚Äôata mayde nder duuniyaaru nde ngol…óum wa…óaaka. Yamiroore nde wa…ói weltini ko haala Allah woni iyol nguurndam, e seltude daga …óum woni seltude to mayde.
elements.questionHint2: Caggal hokki haramtaare nde, Allah wi‚Äôi gorko on waawi nyaami daga fuu le…ó…óe go…ó…óe bee farta≈ã≈ãe ‚Äî …óume faaynude nguu hollata dow no Allah wa‚Äôi nde o fu…ó…óita iyol ngol, e …óum accita fammude haramtaare gooto nde?
elements.sampleAnswer3: God gave a generous permission ‚Äî the man could freely eat from every tree of the orchard ‚Äî alongside a single clear prohibition: he must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. The command was not restrictive in any harsh sense; the one forbidden tree was set against the backdrop of abundant provision. The consequence attached to disobedience was death: ‚Äòyou will surely die.‚Äô This was not a threat but a statement of consequence ‚Äî crossing this boundary would bring death into a world where it had not previously existed. The command established that God‚Äôs word was the boundary of life, and to step outside it was to step towards death.
elements.questionHint3: Before giving the one prohibition, God said the man could ‚Äôfreely eat‚Äô from every other tree ‚Äî what does that generous framing tell you about the kind of God who was setting this boundary, and does that change how you read the single restriction?

He said to the woman, ‚ÄòIs it really true that God said, ‚ÄòYou must not eat from any tree of the orchard‚Äô?‚Äô. . . <sup>6</sup>When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for imparting wisdom, she took some of its fruit and ate; and she also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate. <sup>7</sup>Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they knew they were naked; so they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

elements.sampleAnswer1: Dabara ta maciji ita ce shuka shakku game da kalmar Allah ‚Äî ta fara da tambaya wanda ta canza ainihin abin da Allah ya ce (‚ÄòKo Allah ya ce ba za ku ci daga wani itace a gonar ba?‚Äô), sannan ya nuna cewa Allah na da niyya ta boye a cikin umarnin. Dabara ta tafi daga tambaya game da kalmar Allah zuwa tambaya game da alherin Allah. Dalilai uku suka ∆ôarfafa matar ta ci ‚Äôya‚Äôyan itacen: ta ga shi mai kyau don abinci (sha‚Äôawar jiki), abin farha ga ido (son kyau), da kuma abin marmari domin samun hikima (niyyar zama fiye da haka). Wa…óannan dalilai uku ‚Äî sha‚Äôawa, kyau, da girman kai ‚Äî suna wakiltar tsarin jaraba na …óan adam wanda ya ci gaba har yau.
elements.questionHint1: Mataki na farko na macijin ba shi ne cewa ‚Äôsa…ìa wa Allah‚Äô ba sai dai ya yi tambaya wanda ya sa a yi shakku game da abin da Allah ya ce ‚Äî me ya sa sa mutum ya yi shakku game da amincin kalmar Allah mataki mai tasiri na farko don sa shi ya sa…ìa wa Allah?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Nguurngu mbo…ó…ìe woni fu…ó…óugo shakkugo dow haala Allah ‚Äî fu…ó…óugo e ∆¥amre nde wo…ó…óitii ko Allah haali goonga (‚ÄòKo goonga Allah wi‚Äôi, ha on nyaama daga fuu le…ó…óe nder laabu?‚Äô), nden faayi wi‚Äôugo Allah nani niyye go…ó…óe nder yamiroore nde. Nguurngu nguu yalti daga shakkugo dow haala Allah e shakkugo dow mo∆¥∆¥ere Allah. Niyye …ói…ói sar…ói debbo on nyamugo …ìi…ì…ìe le…ó…óe …óen: o yi‚Äôi …óum mo∆¥…ìi nyamugo (hankuure reedu), belni gite (hi…óde kyawu), e kadi yi…ói ngam he…ìugo hikma (√±aagaari he…ìugo fii heewiri). ∆äe niyye …ói…ói …óe tati ‚Äî hankuure, kyawu, e mawninki hoore ‚Äî hollata nguurngu jarraare ne…ó…óo …ìurngo fayde e …ìuri siftaade.
elements.questionHint2: Fetel araneel mbo…ó…ìe naa‚Äô wi‚Äôugo ‚Äôsa…ìu Allah‚Äô tan, ammaa ∆¥amrugo ∆¥amre nde …ìoorniti ko Allah wi‚Äôi ‚Äî ngam …óume wa…óugo ne…ó…óo shakkugo dow goonga haala Allah woni fetel feewnu…óo araneetol ngam wa…óugo mo sa…ìu …óum?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The serpent‚Äôs strategy was to sow doubt about God‚Äôs word ‚Äî beginning with a question that subtly distorted what God had actually said (‚ÄòDid God really say you must not eat from any tree?‚Äô), and then implying that God had an ulterior motive in giving the command. The tactic moved from questioning God‚Äôs word to questioning God‚Äôs goodness. The woman‚Äôs motivations for eating the fruit were threefold: she saw it was good for food (physical appetite), a delight to the eyes (aesthetic desire), and desirable for gaining wisdom (the ambition to be more). These three motivations ‚Äî appetite, beauty, and pride ‚Äî represent a recognisably human pattern of temptation that has continued ever since.
elements.questionHint3: The serpent‚Äôs first move was not to say ‚Äôdisobey God‚Äô but to ask a question that cast doubt on what God had said ‚Äî why is making someone question the reliability of God‚Äôs word an effective first step towards getting them to disobey it?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Yesu ya ∆ôayyade asalin zunubi ∆ôwarai daga cikin zuciyar …óan adam. Mugunta ba ta shiga cikinmu daga waje ba da farko ‚Äî tana fitowa daga cikinmu. Wannan yana ∆ôalubalanci ra‚Äôayin mashahuri cewa mutane masu kyau ne a zahiri kuma kawai muhallin wuya, tarbiyyar mara kyau, ko yanayin karya na waje ne ke sa su mugu. Ko da yake wa…óannan abubuwa na iya tsara yadda zunubi ke bayyana kansa, Yesu ya ce ba su ne asalinsa ba. Asalin shine zuciyar …óan adam da kanta. Wannan shi ne musunbari mai tada tunani domin yana nufin ba za a iya warware matsalar da kawai inganta yanayi, ilimi, ko tsarin zamantakewa ba ‚Äî tushe yana cikin mutum, kuma yana bu∆ôatar mafita da ta fito daga ciki.

elements.sampleAnswer2: Yeesu holliti asli junuuba woni nder …ìernde ne…ó…óo. Ko kallu…óum naa‚Äô nattataa nder mee…óen daga yaasi ‚Äî …óum wurtotoo daga nder mee…óen. ∆äum ja…ìitata miijo wo…ó…óitiingo wonde yim…ìe bo…ó…ìe e nder ma…ì…ìe, sey tigi e boli muusum, tasee muusum naa‚Äô duuniyaaru bollunde nde ngartira …óe nder halkiinde. Ko …óe ndonngondiri faaynoo…ói nguurngu junuuba, Yeesu wi‚Äôi naa‚Äô …óen asliji …óen. Asliji woni …ìernde ne…ó…óo hoore maajum. ∆äum muusna ngam wi‚Äôata ko mbaasi wa…óude kaw…óol mo∆¥…ìude yonkire, ekkolal naa‚Äô siforde yimiijo tan ndim…óinta ‚Äî hoore wiinire woni nder ne…ó…óo, …óum …ìuri yi…óude hisna daga nder hoore maajum.
elements.questionHint2: Yim…ìe heew…ìe mbayi ko …óum ne…ó…óo rokki tasee mo∆¥…ìere e duuniyaaru laaw…óere, yim…ìe nja…ìirta mo∆¥…ìude e nder ma…ì…ìe tan ‚Äî no Yeesu hollita nder batu ngatu yidde naa‚Äô jaa…ìaande e miijo ngoo, e …óume …óum wa…óata?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus locates the origin of sin firmly within the human heart. Evil does not primarily come into us from outside ‚Äî it comes out of us from within. This directly challenges the popular idea that people are basically good and only made bad by difficult circumstances, poor upbringing, or a corrupt environment. While those factors may shape how sin expresses itself, Jesus says they are not its source. The source is the human heart itself. This is a sobering diagnosis because it means the problem cannot be solved simply by improving conditions, education, or social structures ‚Äî the root is internal, and it requires an internal solution.
elements.questionHint3: Many people believe that if you could give everyone a good upbringing and a fair environment, people would naturally behave well ‚Äî how does Jesus‚Äô diagnosis in this passage agree or disagree with that assumption, and what are the implications either way?

elements.answer1: Ga mutane da yawa, ‚Äòzunubi‚Äô yana tuna mana da jerin munanan ayyuka - kisan kai, sata, da manyan laifuka. Ta wannan ma‚Äôaunin, yawancinmu muna jin cewa ba masu zunubi ba ne. Amma Littafi Mai Tsarki ya fi amfani da kalmar sosai. Zunubi shine kowane tunani, kalma, ko aiki da ya gaza mizanin Allah - kuma, fiye da haka, hali ne na zuciya wanda ke sanya kai a tsakiya maimakon Allah. Tawaye ne ga ikon Allah akan rayuwarmu, sau da yawa a cikin natsuwa da kuma hanyoyin girmamawa. Idan muka fahimci zunubi ta wannan hanyar, da‚Äôawar Littafi Mai Tsarki cewa ‚Äôduka sun yi zunubi‚Äô (Romawa 3:23) ta fara yin ma‚Äôana sosai.
elements.answer2: E yim…ìe heew…ìe, ‚Äòbakkaat‚Äô ina addana …ìe miijo doggol golle bon…óe tee≈ãtu…óe ‚Äî warngooji, gujjugol, bonanndeeji maw…ói. E oon peeje, ko …ìuri heewde e men ina nji…ói hoolaare laa…ìtunde wonde en ngonaa bakkodin…ìe. Kono Biibal ina huutoroo konngol ngol no feewi. Bakkaat ko kala miijo, konngol, walla golle …óe ≈ãakkaani e tolno Alla ‚Äî kadi, ko …ìuri …óum, ko jikku …ìernde jogor…óo wa…óde hoore mum e hakkundeere wonaa Alla. Ko murto e laamu Alla e nguurndam men, heewi wonde ko e laabi dee∆¥…ói, teddu…ói. So en paamii bakkaat nii, haala Biibal ngal wonde ‚Äòfof wa…óii bakkaat‚Äô (Roomnaa…ìe 3:23) fu…ó…óotoo ko …ìeydaade faamde.
elements.answer3: For many people, ‚Äòsin‚Äô brings to mind a list of particularly bad actions ‚Äî murder, theft, serious crimes. By that measure, most of us feel fairly confident we are not sinners. But the Bible uses the word much more broadly. Sin is any thought, word, or action that falls short of God‚Äôs standard ‚Äî and, more than that, it is an attitude of the heart that places self at the centre rather than God. It is rebellion against God‚Äôs authority over our lives, often in quiet, respectable ways. When we understand sin this way, the Bible‚Äôs claim that ‚Äôall have sinned‚Äô (Romans 3:23) starts to make a great deal more sense.

elements.sampleAnswer1: Martanin Allah na ba∆ôin ciki ne mai zurfi da fushin …ìacin rai ‚Äî ya damu da ya yi …óan adam kuma ya …ìace masa ∆ôwarai game da abin da ya gani. elements.sampleAnswer3: God‚Äôs response is one of grief and deep offence ‚Äî He regretted making humankind and was highly offended by what He saw. These are strong emotional terms that tell us sin is not a matter of indifference to God. He is not a detached observer who shrugs at human wickedness; He is personally grieved by it. This matters because it shows that sin is not primarily about breaking rules ‚Äî it is about breaking relationship. God made human beings for closeness with Himself, and seeing them given over entirely to evil (‚Äòonly evil all the time‚Äô) causes Him genuine pain. The depth of His emotional response is a measure of how seriously He takes sin.
elements.questionHint3: The passage says God ‚Äôregretted‚Äô making humankind and was ‚Äôhighly offended‚Äô ‚Äî what do those emotional words tell you about whether God sees sin as a minor inconvenience or something that genuinely affects Him?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness. This is not a future threat only but something already being revealed ‚Äî God‚Äôs settled, holy opposition to sin is an active reality in the world now. To ‚Äôsuppress the truth‚Äô means to hold down or restrain what one knows to be true ‚Äî not ignorance, but deliberate resistance to truth that is available. Paul‚Äôs argument in the surrounding verses is that the existence and nature of God is visible in creation, so that people are without excuse. Suppressing the truth means choosing to push away what can be known about God in order to live without reference to Him.
elements.questionHint3: The word ‚Äôsuppress‚Äô implies that the truth is already present and known ‚Äî you cannot suppress something you have never encountered. What does that tell you about Paul‚Äôs assumption regarding how much people already know about God before they are told the gospel?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul‚Äôs claim is deliberately absolute and universal ‚Äî ‚Äònot even one‚Äô leaves no room for exceptions. Many people‚Äôs instinctive reaction is that this feels too sweeping: surely some people are genuinely good? But Paul is not saying no one ever does anything kind or admirable. He is saying that no one, measured against God‚Äôs standard, is righteous ‚Äî no one fully seeks God, no one consistently does what is right in God‚Äôs eyes. The phrase ‚Äôfall short of the glory of God‚Äô in verse 23 captures this: it is not that some people fall slightly short and others massively so ‚Äî all of us fall short. The point is not to rank human behaviour but to establish that the problem of sin is universal, which is the necessary foundation for understanding why the gospel applies to everyone.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says ‚Äônot even one‚Äô is righteous ‚Äî he is not comparing people to each other but to God‚Äôs standard. How might the conclusion change if the measuring stick is God‚Äôs perfect character rather than a comparison with other human beings?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Bulus ya ce sakamakon ‚Äî albashi da aka samu ‚Äî na zunubi mutuwa ce. Yana amfani da harshen albashi da gangan: mutuwa ba azaba ta bazata ba ce sai dai canjin adalci na rayuwa ta zunubi, abin da zunubi ke cancanta kuma ya samu. Bambancin da ya zana a ∆ôarshen aya shine mai ban mamaki kuma an gina shi da kyau: a kan albashin zunubi (wani abu da aka samu da aka cancanta) ya sanya kyautar Allah (wani abu da aka ba da kyauta ba a samu ba). Mutuwa abin da muke samu ne; rai madawwami abin da Allah ke bayarwa ne. Gefen biyu na aya ba za su iya bambanta fiye da haka ba ‚Äî …óayan adalci ne, …óayan alheri ne. Dukkansu sun tattara cikin mutumtakar Yesu Almasihu, cikin sunansa aka ba da kyautar.
elements.questionHint1: Bulus da gangan ya yi amfani da kalmar ‚Äôsakamakon‚Äô ko ‚Äôalbashi‚Äô don abin da zunubi ke haifar ‚Äî wani abu da aka samu ‚Äî sannan ya kwatanta shi da ‚Äôkyauta‚Äô don abin da Allah ke bayarwa. elements.sampleAnswer2: Bulus wi‚Äôi njo…ìaari ‚Äî ko al…ìasal he…ìaa…óo ‚Äî junuuba …óum mayde. O huutori haala al…ìasal bee annde: mayde naa‚Äô daawal walaa laawol tan ammaa ko njo…ìaari goonga yonki junuuba, ko junuuba fayi e he…ìugo. Sendinnde nde o wa…ói e sakkitorde ayaare nde‚Äôe woni toowndi e feewnaama: fawaade e njo…ìaari junuuba (go…ó…óum he…ìaa…óo e yi…óiraangal) o dawi bii caahu Allah (go…ó…óum hokkaangal bee farta≈ã≈ãe e he…ìaaki). Mayde ko men he…ìirta; yonki ki re‚Äôataa ko Allah hokkata. ∆Åolal …ói…óal ayaare nde‚Äôe naa‚Äô mbaawi sendinde haa …óon ‚Äî gooto ko ladal, go…ó…óo ko mo∆¥∆¥ere. E …ói…óon …ìe ngontondiri e ne…ó…óaagu Yeesu Almasiihu, nder innde muu…óum caahu nguu hokketee.
elements.questionHint2: Bulus e annde huutori mbele ‚Äônjo…ìaari‚Äô naa ko junuuba wa…óata ‚Äî go…ó…óum he…ìaa…óo ‚Äî nden sendini …óum e ‚Äôcaahu‚Äô ngam ko Allah hokkata. Ngam …óume sendinnde hakkunde he…ìugo e he…ìugo ko caahu to…ó…ói ko o wi‚Äôata?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says the payoff ‚Äî the earned wage ‚Äî of sin is death. He uses the language of wages deliberately: death is not an arbitrary punishment but the just return for a life of sin, what sin deserves and earns. The contrast he draws at the end of the verse is striking and carefully constructed: against the wages of sin (something earned and deserved) he places the gift of God (something freely given and unearned). Death is what we earn; eternal life is what God gives. The two sides of the verse could not be more different ‚Äî one is justice, the other is grace. And both are concentrated in the person of Jesus Christ, in whose name the gift is given.
elements.questionHint3: Paul deliberately uses the word ‚Äôpayoff‚Äô or ‚Äôwages‚Äô for what sin produces ‚Äî something that is earned ‚Äî and then contrasts it with a ‚Äôgift‚Äô for what God offers. Why is that distinction between earning and receiving as a gift so important to the point he is making?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes an experience many people recognise: the frustrating gap between knowing what is right and actually doing it, between intentions and actions. He does not describe a person indifferent to goodness ‚Äî he describes someone who genuinely wants to do good and delights in God‚Äôs law, yet finds an opposing force within them winning the battle. His cry of ‚Äôwretched man that I am!‚Äô is one of the most honest outbursts in the Bible. Crucially, he does not look to willpower, self-discipline, or moral effort for rescue ‚Äî his question is ‚Äôwho will rescue me?‚Äô, not ‚Äôwhat must I do?‚Äô The answer is immediate: rescue comes through Jesus Christ. The solution to the internal battle is not trying harder but being rescued by another.
elements.questionHint3: Paul frames his question as ‚Äôwho will rescue me?‚Äô rather than ‚Äôwhat should I do to improve myself?‚Äô ‚Äî what does the choice of that word ‚Äôrescue‚Äô tell you about whether Paul thinks the answer to sin lies within himself or outside himself?

elements.text3: The picture here is bleak ‚Äî but it is honest. We cannot fix this ourselves. The good news is that God does not leave us without hope, which is exactly where the next study begins.

‚ÄôMatar ta ga yadda itacen yana da kyau, ‚Äòya‚Äôyansa kuma kyawawa, abin sha‚Äôawa ne kuma ga ido, abin marmari ne domin ba da hikima, ta tsinka daga ‚Äôya‚Äôyansa, ta kuwa ci, ta kuma bai wa mijinta wa…óansu, shi kuma ya ci.‚Äô

elements.text3:
Sin is rebellion against God. It came into the world through the first man and woman, Adam and Eve, as they deliberately disobeyed God.

‚ÄôWhen the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it.‚Äô ‚Äî Genesis 3:6

elements.text1: A binciken mu na ∆ôarshe mun koyi abinda Allah ya ke tunani game da zunubi da kuma sakamakon zunubin mu. Cikin wannan binciken zamu fara duba shirin Allah domin ceton mu, maimakon hukunta mu.
elements.text2: Nder jannde men …ìennunde, en nji‚Äôii mawnugo junuuba e ko o danyata. Nandola man …óonno ni…ì…ìi. Ammaa Allah yoppaayi en …óon. Jannde nde‚Äôe woni hoto habaru bel…óo fu…ó…óata ‚Äî en …óon ndaara feere Allah mawnunde, naa‚Äô ngam o jukka en, ammaa ngam o hisna en.
elements.text3: In the last study we saw the seriousness of sin and its consequences. The picture was bleak. But God does not leave us there. This study is where the good news begins ‚Äî we look at God‚Äôs extraordinary plan not to punish us, but to rescue us.

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>‚ÄôSaboda ∆ôaunar da Allah ya yi wa duniya har ya ba da maka…óaicin ∆äansa, domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada ya hallaka, sai dai ya sami rai madawwami.
elements.passageText2: <sup>16</sup>‚ÄúGama Allah ya ∆ôaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da maka…óaicin ∆äansa, domin dukan wanda ya ba da gaskiya gare shi kada yƒÉ halaka, amma yƒÉ sami rai na har abada.
elements.passageText3: <sup>16</sup>Gama Allah ya ∆ôaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da wanda yake ∆äaya kuma Maka…óaicin ∆äansa domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada yƒÉ hallaka sai dai yƒÉ sami rai madawwami.
elements.passageText4: <sup>16</sup>Ngam yidde nde Allah yi…ói duuniyaaru haa o hokkii ∆Åi…ó…óo maako bajjo: Bajjo woni …ìi…ó…óo danyaa…óo kanko tan. O walaa minyiraawo koo mawniraawo. , ngam koo moye koolii…óomo to‚Äô halka ammaa he…ìa yonki ki re‚Äôataa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life.

elements.question1: Wannan aya tana ce mana me game da halin Allah game da duniya, duk da zunubin da muka duba a cikin binciken da ya gabata?
elements.question2: ∆äume ayaare nde‚Äôe wolwata dow no Allah yi‚Äôata duuniyaaru, koo nde en ngi‚Äôii mawnugo junuuba nder jannde …ìennunde?
elements.question3: What does this verse tell us about God‚Äôs attitude towards the world, despite the sin we looked at in the last study?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Duk da zunubin duniya, halin Allah yana nuna ∆ôauna mai zurfi ‚Äî ba fushi ko ∆ôi ba. ∆òaunarsa ta kasance mai girma sosai har ta sa shi ya yi aiki: ya ba da mafi ∆ôarancin abu da ya mallaka, wato maka…óaicin ∆äansa. Kuma manufar wannan kyauta ba azaba ba ce, sai dai ceto ‚Äî domin duk wanda ya gaskata kada ya hallaka sai ya sami rai madawwami. Don haka, amsa Allah ga zunubin …óan adam ba yanke hukunci ba ce, sai dai tayin ceto.
elements.questionHint1: Yohanna ya yi amfani da wacce kalma don nuna ∆ôarfin ∆ôaunar Allah, kuma wannan ∆ôauna ta sa shi ya yi me?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Koo dow mawnugo junuuba duuniyaaru, haala Allah woni yidde mawnunde ‚Äî wano kuu…ìungal koo haylugal. Yidde maako mawnii haa o wa…ói golle: o hokkii ko …ìuri teddugo e nder ko o arani, ∆Åi…ó…óo maako bajjo. Nden anniya ko hokki …óum waa‚Äô jukkere, ammaa hisneede ‚Äî ngam koo moye koolii…óo to halkataa ammaa he…ìa yonki ngiinaaka. Laawol Allah joofingal e hakke ne…ó…óo …óum, to wonaa kiita, ammaa hokkoore kisinii.
elements.questionHint2: ∆äume kalma Yahaya ju…ì…ìinii hollitugol fii sabaabu Allah wa…ói, nden yidde nde‚Äôe yahii haa …óume?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Despite the world‚Äôs sin, God‚Äôs attitude is one of deep love ‚Äî not anger or rejection. His love was so great that it moved him to action: he gave the most precious thing he had, his one and only Son. And the purpose of that gift was not punishment but rescue ‚Äî so that anyone who believes would not perish but have eternal life. God‚Äôs response to human sinfulness is therefore not condemnation but an offer of salvation.
elements.questionHint3: What word does John use to describe God‚Äôs motivation, and what did that motivation lead God to do?

elements.passageText5: <sup>45</sup>For even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

elements.question1: Ta yaya Yesu ya bayyana nufin zuwansa? Kalmar ‚Äôfansa‚Äô tana nuna mana me game da halinmu?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Yesu ya bayyana nufin zuwansa a matsayin ba ya zo domin a bauta masa ba, sai dai domin ya yi bauta, musamman ya ba da ransa fansa saboda mutane da yawa. Kalmar ‚Äôfansa‚Äô tana nuna cewa muna cikin yanayin bauta ko bashi da ba za mu iya biyansa da kanmu ba ‚Äî an …óaure wani, kuma dole a biya farashi don a ‚Äôyantar da shi. Idan aka yi amfani da wannan ga mu, yana nufin muna da …óaurewa saboda zunubi da hukumcinsa, kuma Yesu da son ransa ya ba da ransa a matsayin ku…óin fansa wanda ya ‚Äôyantar da mu.
elements.questionHint1: Yi tunani game da yadda ake amfani da fansa a rayuwar yau da kullum ‚Äî wannan yana nuna mana me game da halin mutanen da Yesu ya zo don taimakonsu?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Yeesu siffitirii anniya maako bana o warii naa‚Äô ngam o jagganee, ammaa ngam o jagganoo yim…ìe, kadi ngam o hokka yonki maako rim…óinki …óu…ó…ìe. Kalma ‚Äôrim…óinki‚Äô hollata en ngo…ó…óen e yanaaji jippaaki koo bornuuki ngam mee…óen ke…ìataa sooditugol ‚Äî ne…ó…óo jogitaama, farilla ke…ìetee ngam yo…ìooyrude mo. To nde …óum yottiniraa mee…óen, …óum hollata en ngon…óen nder jippaari junuuba e jukkere nde junuuba danyata, nden Yeesu e hol hoore maako hokkii yonki maako ngam kuugal ngal winnditini en.
elements.questionHint2: Miijo dow …óume rim…óinki wa…óiraa ngam nder nguurndam hannde ‚Äî …óume …óum hollata en dow alhaali yim…ìe …ìen Yeesu warii wallitud…ìe?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus describes his purpose as coming not to be served but to serve, and specifically to give his life as a ransom for many. The word ‚Äôransom‚Äô implies that we are in a state of captivity or debt that we cannot pay ourselves ‚Äî someone is held, and a price must be paid to secure their release. Applied to us, it means we are bound by sin and its penalty, and Jesus willingly gave his own life as the payment that sets us free.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what a ransom is used for in everyday life ‚Äî what does that tell you about the condition of the people Jesus came to help?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Amma kuwa Allah yana tabbatar mana da ∆ôaunar da yake yi mana, wato tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya tabbatar mana yawan ∆ôaunarsa, da yake tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu saboda mu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya nuna mana ∆ôaunarsa a wannan. Tun muna masu zunubi tukuna, Kiristi ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>Ammaa laawol ngol Allah hollii‚Äôen yidde maako woni, carel ko ngon…óen nder hakke, Almasiihu maayii ngam mee…óen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question1: Me ya sa ∆ôaunar Allah a nan ta yi fice musamman, bisa ga abin da Bulus ya fa…óa?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Abin da ya sa ∆ôaunar Allah ta yi fice musamman shi ne lokacin da aka nuna ta: Almasihu ya mutu dominmu tun muna masu zunubi tukuna. Ba mu gyara halayenmu ba, ba mu juya zuwa ga Allah ba, kuma ba mu samu tagomashi nasa ta wata hanya ba. Yawanci muna iya tsammani cewa wani zai yiwa mutumin mai kyau ko mai cancanta sadaukarwa ‚Äî amma Allah ya nuna ∆ôaunarsa a daidai lokacin da ba mu cancanta ta ba. Wannan ∆ôauna ba ta da sharadi kuma ta fito ne daga Allah …óaya ka…óai; tana fitowa ne daga halin Allah, ba daga kome a cikinmu ba.
elements.questionHint1: Lura da kalmar ‚Äôtun‚Äô ‚Äî a wane lokaci na halinmu na …óabi‚Äôa ne Almasihu ya mutu, kuma don me wannan lokacin yana da muhimmanci?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Ko wa…óii yidde Allah …ìur…ói mawnugo woni sahaa nde holliraa: carel ko ngon…óen nder hakke, Almasiihu maayii ngam mee…óen. En ngoppaaki golle …ìur…óe, en ngurtaaki haa Allah, kadi en ke…ìaaki barke maako e laawol fuu. Ko addata, ko tawata ne…ó…óo wadday hoore maako ngam ne…ó…óo mo∆¥∆¥o koo woo…ó…óo yo…ìde ‚Äî ammaa Allah hollii yidde maako e sahaa nde ngon…óen maa …ìur…ói too…óugo. Yidde nde‚Äôe waa‚Äô e haala e tagoore; …óum yo…ì…ìii daga …ìernde Allah, naa daga ko fuu e mee…óen.
elements.questionHint2: Firo kalma ‚Äôcarel‚Äô ‚Äî to huunde bonde ngon…óon e nder …óuum nde Almasiihu maayii, nden ngam …óume sahaa nde‚Äôe woodani?
elements.sampleAnswer3: What makes God‚Äôs love striking is the timing: Christ died for us while we were still sinners. We had not cleaned up our act, turned to God, or earned his favour in any way. Normally we might expect someone to sacrifice themselves for a good or deserving person ‚Äî but God demonstrated his love at the very point when we were least deserving of it. The love is unconditional and entirely one-directional; it comes from God‚Äôs character, not from anything in us.
elements.questionHint3: Notice the word ‚Äôwhile‚Äô ‚Äî at what point in our moral condition did Christ die, and why does the timing matter?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Maganar nan tabbatacciya ce, ta kuma cancanci a kar…ìe ta …óungum cewa, ‚ÄúAlmasihu Yesu ya shigo duniya ne domin ceton masu zunubi,‚Äú ni ne kuwa babbansu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Maganar nan tabbatacciya ce, kuma ta isa a kar…ìe ta gaba …óaya cewa, ‚ÄúAlmasihu Yesu ya zo duniya domin ceton masu zunubi,‚Äù a cikinsu duka ni ne mafi zunubi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>15</sup>Ga wata magana tabbatacciya wadda ta cancanci cikakkiyar kar…ìa. Kiristi Yesu ya zo duniya domin ceton masu zunubi, wanda nake mafi muni a cikinsu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>15</sup>Yeesu Almasiihu warii nder duuniyaaru ngam hisna ga…óoo…ìe hakke. Haala ka‚Äôa ka tabbutuka ka hewtii …óum ja…ìaka. Min boo, mi …ìurii koo moye wa…óuki hakke.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance: ‚ÄòChrist Jesus came into the world to save sinners‚Äô‚Äîand I am the worst of them!

elements.question1: Bulus ya kira kansa ‚Äômafi muni‚Äô a cikin masu zunubi. Don me kuke tsammanin ya fa…ói haka? Wannan yana nuna mana me game da mutanen da Yesu ya zo don cetarsu?
elements.question2: Bulus noddi hoore maako ‚Äô…ìur…óo mawnugo‚Äô junuuba. Ngam …óume a miijata o wolwi …óum? ∆äume …óum hollata en dow yim…ìe …ìe Yeesu wari hisnugo?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Bulus mai yiwuwa yana magana game da zamaninsa na tsanantawa a kan Kiristoci da ∆ôarfi ‚Äî wanda ya bi da kuma tsare mabiya Yesu. Bai fa…ói haka don karya tawali‚Äôu ba, sai dai don ya nuna wata muhimmiyar hujja: idan Almasihu ya zo don cetarsa ma, to babu wanda ya wuce iyaka. Wannan yana nuna mana cewa Yesu bai zo don cetarwa masu kyaun …óabi‚Äôa kawai ko masu zunubi ka…óan ba ‚Äî ya zo don ceton masu zunubi, a ta∆ôaice. Fa…óin ceto yana da girma kamar zurfi na zunubin …óan adam.
elements.questionHint1: Idan ko ‚Äômafi muni‚Äô a cikin masu zunubi an ha…óa shi cikin nufin ceton Almasihu, to wannan yana nuna me game da ko wani zai iya …óaukaka ya wuce iyaka saboda zunubinsa?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Bulus fii …ìuri gooto to o woni ga…óo Almasiihu …ìe, ne…ó…óo torrinoo…óo goon…óin…ìe e baw…óe ‚Äî …óum woni o yaa…ìirii Yeesu goon…óin…ìe ngonno…óo o …óaggoo …ìe e kaso. O wolwaa‚Äô …óum ngam fenan…óam, ammaa ngam o hollita haala mawnuunde: to Almasiihu warii hisnude kadi mo, to walaa mo yahrataa oon falnde. ∆äum hollata en Yeesu waraayi tan ngam hisnude cenii…ìe koo goon…óin…ìe semmbe ‚Äî o warii ngam hisna ga…óoo…ìe hakke, …óum …ìuri fuu. Fa…ói kisineeji …ìurii mawnugo bana mawnugo junuuba yim…ìe.
elements.questionHint2: To kadi ‚Äô…ìur…óo mawnugo‚Äô e hakke kawtaama nder anniya Almasiihu kisinirde, …óume …óum hollata e woodugol ne…ó…óo …ìur…ìe mawnugo e hakke haa o hisinataake?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul is probably referring to his past as a violent persecutor of Christians ‚Äî someone who had actively hunted down and imprisoned followers of Jesus. He does not say this to be falsely modest, but to make a point: if Christ came to save even him, then no one is beyond reach. It tells us that Jesus did not come only for the morally respectable or the mildly flawed ‚Äî he came to save sinners, full stop. The scope of salvation is as wide as the depth of human sinfulness.
elements.questionHint3: If even the ‚Äôworst‚Äô sinner is included in Christ‚Äôs saving purpose, what does that imply about whether anyone could be considered too sinful to be saved?

elements.sampleAnswer3: According to Romans 5:9, those who trust in Christ are declared righteous ‚Äî a legal verdict made possible by Jesus‚Äô blood (his atoning death). And as a result of that declaration, they are saved from God‚Äôs wrath. This is significant: God‚Äôs wrath is his righteous judgment against sin, which we all deserve. But through Christ, that judgment has been dealt with, and believers are no longer under its threat.

elements.sampleAnswer3: The believer is not condemned ‚Äî the verdict against them has been lifted through faith in Jesus. elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes their fate as ‚Äôeternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his strength.‚Äô This is not annihilation but permanent separation from God ‚Äî the source of all goodness, light, and life. It is a sobering description, and many people find it deeply uncomfortable. It raises hard questions about fairness and the character of God. Yet Paul frames it within God‚Äôs righteous justice: it is a right response to those who have rejected both God and the gospel. How it sits with us personally will vary, and that honest reaction is worth exploring.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses a specific phrase to describe what this destruction means relationally ‚Äî what are those people separated from, and why might that detail matter more than the word ‚Äôdestruction‚Äô alone?

Kiita ndee waajudii dow mee…óen ngam junuubi mee…óen wa…ói dow makko e …óum addani mo anndugol ko mee…óen njaarake.
elements.questionHint2: To en ngi‚Äôii ko Bulus hollitii dow sakaare junuuba (ittugol daga Allah), …óume …óum hollita nde Yeesu anndii ittugol gootol oo dow gaafaangal?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus cries out ‚ÄôMy God, my God, why have you forsaken me?‚Äô ‚Äî a cry of real abandonment, not just distress. For the eternal Son who had always existed in perfect unity with the Father, this forsakenness represents something almost unthinkable: a rupture in that relationship. Theologically, it points to Jesus bearing the full weight of sin‚Äôs consequence ‚Äî separation from God ‚Äî in our place. The judgment that should fall on us for our sin fell on him instead, which is why he experienced what we deserved.
elements.questionHint3: Given what we have already seen about the consequence of sin (separation from God), what might it mean that Jesus experienced that same separation on the cross?

elements.sampleAnswer1: Bulus ya bayyana musanyar biyu mai ban mamaki. Yesu, wanda ba shi da zunubinsa na kansa (‚Äòwanda bai san zunubi ba‚Äô), an mai da shi zunubi dominmu ‚Äî ya kar…ìi zunubin namu da duk sakamakonsa. Maimakon haka, masu imani suna samun adalcin Allah ‚Äî ba adalcin da suka samu ba, sai dai wanda ya kasance na Allah kansa, an lissafta masu ta wurin ha…óin kai da Almasihu. Musanya ce ta abubuwa masu bambanci: zunubin namu yana je kansa; adalcin nasa yana zuwa gare mu.
elements.questionHint1: Bulus ya ce Yesu ‚Äôbai san zunubi ba‚Äô ‚Äî don haka abin da ya bayyana ya yi mamaki ta wace hanya, kuma kowane …ìangare na musanyar yana ba da me kuma yana samun me?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Bulus siffitirii waylowaylo maw…óo …ói…ói. Yeesu, …óum mo mee…óaayi wa…óuki hakke (‚ÄòAlmasiihu mee…óaayi wa…óuki hakke‚Äô), Allah yowii fuu hakkeeji mee…óen dow maako ‚Äî o ja…ìi hakkeeji mee…óen e danyeeki mum fuu. E …óum, koolii…ìe ke…ìata aadilaaku Allah ‚Äî naa aadilaaku …ìe ke…ìi, ammaa aadilaaku nanngaa…óo daga Allah hoore maako, tokkaa…óo e ma…ì…ìe e laawol kawtugol e Almasiihu. ∆äum woni waylowaylo …ói…ói bertaa…ói: hakkeeji mee…óen yahata dow makko; aadilaaku maako warta dow mee…óen.
elements.questionHint2: Bulus wi‚Äôi Yeesu ‚Äômee…óaayi wa…óuki hakke‚Äô ‚Äî sabu …óum, …óume wa…óata waylowaylo o siffitirii maw…óo, nden …óume koo fuu nder waylowaylo nde hokkata e ke…ìata?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes a remarkable double exchange. Jesus, who had no sin of his own (‚Äòthe one who did not know sin‚Äô), was made to be sin for us ‚Äî he took on our sin and its full consequence. In return, believers receive the righteousness of God ‚Äî not a righteousness they earned, but one that belongs to God himself, credited to them through union with Christ. It is a swap of opposites: our sin goes to him; his righteousness comes to us.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says Jesus ‚Äôdid not know sin‚Äô ‚Äî so what makes the exchange he describes so surprising, and what does each party in the exchange give and receive?

elements.sampleAnswer3: The curse Paul refers to is the curse of the law ‚Äî the judgment and condemnation that falls on everyone who fails to keep God‚Äôs law perfectly (which is all of us). Christ took that curse on himself by hanging on a cross, which the Old Testament specifically associated with being cursed by God. To ‚Äôredeem‚Äô us means to buy us out of that condition ‚Äî like purchasing the freedom of a slave. Christ paid the price of the curse so that we would no longer be under it.
elements.questionHint3: What does it mean to ‚Äôredeem‚Äô something in everyday life (think of redeeming a voucher or buying something back), and how does that shed light on what Christ did for us?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Peter says Jesus bore our sins in his body on the cross ‚Äî he physically and personally carried them. The purpose was twofold: that we might cease from sinning (freedom from sin‚Äôs power) and live for righteousness (a new positive direction of life). Peter also adds that by Jesus‚Äô wounds we were healed ‚Äî using the language of healing to describe what the cross accomplishes spiritually. The cross is not only about forgiveness of past sin but about transformation for the future.
elements.questionHint3: Peter mentions two results of Jesus bearing our sins ‚Äî one is about stopping something and the other is about starting something. Can you identify what each is?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity. The two ‚Äôones‚Äô are therefore God himself and the mediator ‚Äî Christ Jesus. elements.answer1: ‚ÄôCeto‚Äô na iya yin kama da tsohon harshe na addini, amma kawai yana nufin ceto. Littafi Mai Tsarki ya yi amfani da shi don bayyana abin da Allah ya yi mana ta wurin Yesu - ceton mu daga sakamakon zunubi (hukuncin Allah) da kuma dawo da mu zuwa ga dangantaka mai kyau da Shi. Ceto ba abu ne da muke cimmawa ta hanyar kasancewa mai kyau ba; wani abu ne da Allah ya yi kuma ya ba mu kyauta. Samun sa yana bu∆ôatar amsa, wanda shine abin da Bincike na 8 ya bincika.
elements.answer2: ‚ÄôKisal‚Äô ina waawi nanndude e …óemngal diine …ìooyngal, kono firti tan ko hisnude. Deftere nde‚Äôe huutiniri nde ngam hollugo ko Allah wa…ói en bee Yeesu ‚Äî hisnugo en diga batte hakkeeji (kiita Allah) e wartirgo en nder jokkirgal boo…óngal bee maako. Kisal wonaa huunde nde ke…ìten e wonde mo∆¥∆¥u…ìe haa heewi; ko huunde nde Alla wa…ói, rokki en e dow weltaare. Ja…ì…ìaade …óum ina …óa…ì…ìi jaabawol, ko …óum woni ko jannde 8 ndee yuurnitoo.
elements.answer3: ‚ÄôSalvation‚Äô can sound like old-fashioned religious language, but it simply means rescue. The Bible uses it to describe what God has done for us through Jesus ‚Äî rescuing us from the consequences of sin (God‚Äôs judgement) and restoring us to a right relationship with Him. Salvation is not something we achieve through being good enough; it is something God has done and freely offers to us. Receiving it requires a response, which is what Study 8 explores.

elements.text3: The cross is not the end of the story. God the Father raised Jesus from the dead ‚Äî and that changes everything. Christians do not worship a dead teacher; they worship a risen Saviour and living Lord. In this study we explore what the resurrection and ascension mean for us.

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>In kuwa ba a ta da Almasihu ba, to, ashe, wa‚Äôazinmu a banza ne, bangaskiyarku kuma banza ce. <sup>17</sup>Luk 24.34 ;  Mat 28.16 , 17 ;  Mar 16.14 ;  Luk 24.36 ;  Yah 20.19 <sup>18</sup>Ashe, wa…óanda suka yi barci a kan su na Almasihu, sun hallaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>Da begenmu ga Almasihu domin wannan rai ne ka…óai, ai, da mun fi kowa zama abin tausayi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>14</sup>Idan kuwa Almasihu bai tashi daga matattu ba, ashe, waÀàazinmu banza ne, bangaskiyarku kuma ta zama banza. ... <sup>17</sup>Idan kuwa ba a tƒÅ da Almasihu ba, ashe, bangaskiyarku banza ce, har yanzu kuma kuna cikin zunubanku. <sup>18</sup>Ashe, wa…óanda suka mutu cikin Almasihu, sun halaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>Idan sa zuciyarmu ga Almasihu domin rayuwar nan ne ka…óai take, ai, mun fi kowa zama abin tausayi ke nan.
elements.passageText3: <sup>14</sup>In ba a tashe Kiristi ba kuwa, ai, wa‚Äôazinmu, da bangaskiyarku banza ne. <sup>17</sup>In kuwa ba a tashe Kiristi ba, bangaskiyarku a banza take, ya zama har yanzu kuna cikin zunubanku ke nan. <sup>18</sup>Ashe, wa…óanda suka yi barci a cikin Kiristi ma sun hallaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>In a wannan rayuwar ce ka…óai muke da bege cikin Kiristi, mun zama abin tausayi fiye da dukan mutane.
elements.passageText4: <sup>14</sup>To boo Almasiihu ummaaki daga mayde, too, wa‚Äôajinki amin wartii meere, nden hoolaare moo…óon wartii meere. <sup>17</sup>Daa Almasiihu umminaaka, too, hoolaare moo…óon wartii meere, nden haa jooni on ngondi e hakke moo…óon, <sup>18</sup>nden may…ìe e koolii Almasiihu kalkii won …óon. <sup>19</sup>To ni ngam jonde duuniyaaru ndu‚Äôu tan nga…óeten kammunde dow Almasiihu, en …ìurii koo moye wartuki …ìe …óum yurminanta.
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is futile and your faith is empty. . . <sup>17</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is useless; you are still in your sins. <sup>18</sup>Furthermore, those who have fallen asleep in Christ have also perished. <sup>19</sup>For if only in this life we have hope in Christ, we should be pitied more than anyone.

Remember how he told you, while he was still in Galilee, <sup>7</sup>that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again.‚Äô

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says plainly that no one will be declared righteous by observing the law ‚Äî because the law‚Äôs function is not to make us righteous but to reveal that we are not. It acts as a mirror, showing us where we fall short: through the law comes the knowledge of sin. If the law‚Äôs purpose is diagnosis, not cure, then trying to use it as a path to salvation is a category error. It can tell us what is wrong; it cannot fix it.

elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul‚Äôs use of ‚Äôdead‚Äô is deliberate and radical. A dead person cannot improve themselves, choose differently, or contribute to their own rescue ‚Äî they need someone from outside to act on them entirely. Spiritual deadness is therefore a complete obstacle to self-salvation: we lack both the ability and the spiritual life to initiate a response to God from our own resources. The image also challenges the common assumption that people are basically good and capable of reaching God through effort ‚Äî Paul says our starting point is not weakness but death.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what a physically dead person is capable of doing for themselves ‚Äî how does that image apply to what Paul is saying about our spiritual condition before God?

elements.sampleAnswer3: It rules out any human contribution to salvation ‚Äî effort, moral achievement, religious observance, or personal merit. Paul is explicit: it is not from yourselves, it is not from works. The reason no one can boast follows directly from that: boasting requires a personal achievement to point to. If salvation is entirely God‚Äôs gift from start to finish, there is nothing on our side to be proud of. This also levels the ground completely ‚Äî no one is more saved than anyone else because they worked harder or were more deserving.
elements.questionHint3: If someone received a gift and then boasted about it, what would that reveal about how they misunderstood where it came from ‚Äî and how does that apply to salvation?

elements.answer1: E nder kuutoragol √±alnde kala, ‚Äòmo∆¥∆¥ere‚Äô ina waawi firtude ≈ãar…óugol walla poise. E nder deftere nde, …óum firti ko huunde seertunde no feewi: mo∆¥∆¥ere nde fotaani. Nde Bibel wi‚Äôi en kisnaama ‚Äôe mo‚Äôere‚Äô, …óum …óon holla kisndam men …óum dokkal Allah fuu ‚Äî naa njo…ìdi ngam kuugal men, boo…ó…óum men, malla kuugal men diina. ∆äum ina tee≈ãti sabu ina ha…óa kala manngu to bannge men, tee firti ko hay gooto mee…óaa wo…ó…óude he…ìde …óum.
elements.answer2: A amfani da shi na yau da kullum, ‚Äòalheri‚Äô na iya nufin kyau ko kwanciyar hankali. A cikin Littafi Mai Tsarki, yana nufin wani abu daban: alherin da ba mu cancanta ba. Alheri shine Allah yana ba mu abin da ba mu cancanta ba kuma ba za mu iya samu ba. Lokacin da Littafi Mai Tsarki ya ce an cece mu ‚Äôta wurin alheri‚Äô, yana nufin cetonmu gaba …óaya kyautar Allah ce - ba lada ba ne ga ∆ôo∆ôarinmu, alherinmu, ko ayyukanmu na addini. Wannan yana da mahimmanci domin yana hana duk wani alfahari daga …ìangarenmu, kuma yana nufin cewa babu wanda ya ta…ìa yin nisa da kar…ìarsa.
elements.answer3: In everyday use, ‚Äògrace‚Äô can mean elegance or poise. In the Bible, it means something quite different: undeserved favour. Grace is God giving us what we do not deserve and could never earn. When the Bible says we are saved ‚Äôby grace‚Äô, it means our salvation is entirely God‚Äôs gift ‚Äî not a reward for our efforts, our goodness, or our religious activity. This is important because it rules out any boasting on our part, and it means that no one is ever too far gone to receive it.

elements.sampleAnswer1: Umurnin biyu sune: tuba da gaskata bishara. Yesu ya ba su tare domin su ne amsoshi da ba za a iya raba su ba ga wannan gaskiyar guda ‚Äî zuwan Mulkin Allah. Tuba ba tare da bangaskiya ba na nufin juya daga wani abu ba tare da juya zuwa wani abu ba. Bangaskiya ba tare da tuba ba amincewa ne wanda ba ta canza yadda mutum ke rayuwa. Tare suna bayyana juyi cikakke: daga zunubi da nufin kai, zuwa ga Allah da bisharar sa cikin Yesu. Su ne bangarori biyu na amsa guda …óaya, ba jerin matakai daban-daban ba.
elements.questionHint1: Ka/ki yi tunani game da abin da tuba ke nufi da abin da gaskata ke nufi ‚Äî idan ka/ki yi …óaya kawai daga cikin biyu, menene zai kasance gurbi a cikin amsar ka/ki?
elements.sampleAnswer2: Umrooje …ói…ói …óe ngoni: tuubugo e goon…óinugo Habaru Bel…óum on. Yeesu …óon hokka …óum kawtum ngam …ìe ngoni njaabawuuji …óe kaatonoontari e goonga gootongo ‚Äî warugo Laamu Allah. Tuubugo walaa hoolaare woni ngaylito weera ko …óume walaa ngaylito yaade to ko …óume. Hoolaare walaa tuubugo woni goon…óinugo ngal waytaaki no ne…ó…óo nguurnirta. Kawtum …óon sifanta ngaylito he…ìi-he…ìi: weera hakke e nii nguurnirtee e hoore mee…óen, yaade to Allah e Habaru Bel…óum muu…óum e Yeesu. ∆äee ngoni …ói…ói-ango gooto gootongo njaabowo, walaa feere kuuje …ói…ói ceerndu…óe.
elements.questionHint2: Miijo dow ko tuubugo wa…óata e ko goon…óinugo wa…óata ‚Äî to a wa…ói gooto tan diga …ói…ói …óuye, ko woni ko haa…óata nder jaabore ma?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The two commands are: repent and believe the gospel. Jesus gives them together because they are inseparable responses to the same reality ‚Äî the arrival of God‚Äôs kingdom. Repentance without faith would be turning away from something without turning toward anything. Faith without repentance would be a belief that makes no difference to how one lives. Together they describe a complete turning: away from sin and self-direction, and toward God and his good news in Jesus. They are two sides of a single response, not a sequence of separate steps.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what repenting means and what believing means ‚Äî if you only did one of the two, what would be incomplete about your response?

elements.sampleAnswer3: God calls the people to repent, turn from their wicked deeds, and throw away their sins ‚Äî active language that involves deliberate change. But the deepest demand is for ‚Äôa new heart and a new spirit,‚Äò which points to something far more radical than adjusting outward conduct. The heart in biblical thought is the seat of the will, desires, and identity. Repentance therefore reaches beyond behaviour into motive and character ‚Äî it is a transformation of what a person loves and lives for, not merely a change in what they do.
elements.questionHint3: God could have just said ‚Äôstop doing wrong things‚Äô ‚Äî why do you think he goes further and asks for a new heart and a new spirit?

elements.sampleAnswer3: It suggests they are connected but distinct: the inward change comes first and is the root, while the outward deeds are the fruit that grows from it. Repentance is not merely a claim or a feeling ‚Äî it produces visible, consistent change in how a person lives. At the same time, Paul does not say that the deeds are the repentance itself; they are ‚Äôconsistent with‚Äô it, which means they flow from a genuine inner turning rather than being the turning itself. Outward change without inward change would be performance; inward change without any outward effect would raise serious questions about whether the change was real.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says deeds that are ‚Äôconsistent with‚Äô repentance ‚Äî if the deeds are consistent with something, what does that tell you about which comes first, and what produces the other?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Without faith it is impossible to please God. The two things a person must believe in order to approach him are: first, that he exists; and second, that he rewards those who seek him. The first is the foundation ‚Äî you cannot meaningfully approach a God you do not believe is there. The second is equally necessary ‚Äî it implies that God is not indifferent or hostile to those who seek him, but actively responds to them. Both beliefs together make genuine approach to God possible; without either, there is no real basis for seeking him.
elements.questionHint3: The writer gives two beliefs, not one ‚Äî why might it be insufficient to believe that God exists if you did not also believe the second thing?

elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus promises eternal life to everyone who looks to him and believes, and he adds that he will raise them up at the last day ‚Äî their future is secure even beyond death. The fact that this is described as the Father‚Äôs will is significant: it means this is not a plan that could be overruled, revised, or fall through. It also means that when someone comes to faith, they are not gatecrashing an arrangement ‚Äî they are stepping into exactly what the Father has willed for them.

elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says his sheep listen to his voice, he knows them, and they follow him ‚Äî a mutual relationship of recognition and direction on both sides. The security he promises is absolute: he gives them eternal life, they will never perish, and no one can snatch them from his hand. The language is as strong as it could be: not ‚Äôthey are unlikely to be lost‚Äô or ‚Äôthey will be safe if they try hard enough,‚Äò but they will never perish and no one can remove them. The security rests not on the grip of the sheep but on the grip of the shepherd.

elements.sampleAnswer3: The claim is grounded in two related facts: Jesus holds his priesthood permanently because he lives forever, and he always lives to intercede for his people. Unlike any human priest, whose death ends their ministry, Jesus‚Äô priestly work never ceases. ‚ÄôCompletely‚Äô therefore means total and ongoing ‚Äî not just forgiveness of past sins but continuous intercession that covers every situation. The permanence of his priesthood is the foundation of the security of those who trust him: they are not relying on a representative who might stop, fail, or be replaced.
elements.questionHint3: The writer connects ‚Äôsave completely‚Äô to the fact that Jesus ‚Äôalways lives‚Äô ‚Äî what would change about the completeness of the salvation if Jesus‚Äô intercession could ever stop?

elements.text3: Repentance and faith belong together ‚Äî you cannot really have one without the other. To repent is to turn away from sin and from living life our own way. To have faith is to turn toward Jesus and trust Him completely. These are not two separate steps; they are two sides of the same turning.

elements.sampleAnswer1: Wa…óanda aka ceto an ‚Äôyanta su daga zunubi ‚Äî ba shi da i∆ôirarin da ya ke bayyana rayuwarsu kamar yadda ya ta…ìa yi. Amma ‚Äôyanci daga mai iko …óaya yana nufin kasancewa ga wani: sun zama bayin adalci. elements.sampleAnswer2: ∆Åeen hisnaa…ìe ndim…óinaama daga hakke ‚Äî …óum wa…óaa …ìe ko o taa…ìaani he…ìi-he…ìi nder wuurnam ma…ì…ìe hono no o wa…óiri. Ammaa ndim…óinagol diga gootel jom laamu wa…óa woodugol e go…ó…óo: …ìe ngartii maccu…ìe aadilaaku. Bulus huutoo nandolaaru maccu…óo bee nuun…óal ngam hollitde baasal he…ìi-he…ìi e tokkuki nyalnde fuu, naa‚Äô waylugo darnde tan. Taa…ìal ngal, …óum wa…óa ko wuurnam Kirista waayi ko yanaataa dingiral mum ‚Äî daawal ko hakke koo no o nguurnirtoo hoore mum; …óum woni wuurdam bee neli laawol e nuun…óal. Alheri waayi naa‚Äô ruksawu ngam wuurde ko ne…ó…óo yi…ói ‚Äî …óum woni baw…óe nde wa…óa laawol keso wuurdam yiwi kadi e foti.
elements.questionHint2: Bulus wi‚Äôi goon…óin…ìe ‚Äôndim…óinaama daga hakke‚Äô ammaa hannde o …ìeydi …ìe ngoni jooni ‚Äômaccu…ìe aadilaaku‚Äô ‚Äî to …ìe ndim…óinaama daga ko …óume, ngam …óume ndim…óinagol …óoo wa…óa …ìe nattude e go…ó…óum?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Those who have been saved have been freed from sin ‚Äî it no longer has the defining claim over their lives that it once did. But freedom from one master means belonging to another: they have become slaves of righteousness. Paul uses the slavery image deliberately to convey total allegiance and practical daily obedience, not merely a change of status. Practically, it means that the Christian life is not a continuation of the old life with sins forgiven; it is a life actively oriented toward what is right. Grace is not permission to live however one wants ‚Äî it is the power that makes a new direction of life both possible and required.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says believers have been ‚Äôfreed from sin‚Äô but immediately adds that they are now ‚Äôslaves of righteousness‚Äô ‚Äî if they have been freed from one thing, why does that freedom mean being bound to something else?

elements.text1: Zata iya yiwuwar a iya yin binciken Littafi Mai Tsarki a kuma san shi sosai, amma kuma a kasa gane dalilin da yasa aka rubuta shi - da kuma kasa fahimtar yarda zamu samu kyakyawa zaman takewa da Allah.
